parentQuestion: Theoretically, an individual born without a middle ear would be able to hear by bone conduction with a hearing aid.

scaffolds.q: Sound can travel through solid materials, not just air.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Sound travels even better through solids than air — vibrations transfer directly.

scaffolds.q: The skull is made of bone and can transmit vibrations.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Bone conducts sound — that's why your voice sounds different to yourself.

scaffolds.q: The cochlea (inner ear) contains the actual hearing receptor cells.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Hair cells in the cochlea convert sound vibrations into neural signals.

scaffolds.q: The middle ear (ossicles) normally transmits sound from eardrum to cochlea.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Malleus, incus, stapes form the normal air conduction pathway.

scaffolds.q: Bone conduction bypasses the outer and middle ear entirely.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Skull vibrations go directly to the cochlea without needing eardrums or ossicles.

scaffolds.q: Bone conduction hearing aids vibrate the skull to stimulate the cochlea.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! BAHAs (bone-anchored hearing aids) transmit sound through bone, not air.

scaffolds.q: If the inner ear (cochlea) is intact, bone conduction can restore hearing.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Middle ear problems can be bypassed if the cochlea and auditory nerve work.

scaffolds.q: The Weber test uses a tuning fork on the skull to test bone conduction hearing.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Placing a tuning fork on the forehead tests whether bone conduction pathways work.

scaffolds.q: Someone without a middle ear could hear via bone conduction if their inner ear works.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Bone conduction hearing aids vibrate the skull, sending sound directly to the cochlea.
